title: Trading strategy issues
author: CelerySpecial
selftext:
For context, my best, most recent backtesting was at 54% win rate, with about 1.34 sharp and 4k profit over 100 trades across the last 2 years. This pic is just to show what else im working with. Im struggling bringing profit up. I see many other models making some crazy profits and im thinking im not doing smth right. I have decent profits compared to what im making trading on my own but i think it could be a lot better. My current strategy is making day trades and swing trades (2-7 days). And it uses many indicators and analyzes the charts and scores each indicator based on how close it is to the optimal value with the corresponding candle, ex 1 minute, 1h, etc. I just cant seem to get the backtesting anywhere near where I want it. Large portion of trades end up hitting the hold limit, not hitting the stop or the targets. Ive implemented backtesting sweeping which tests multiple different values for each indicator and different combos for them to find the best strat but that only helped a little bit. Im working on getting better api’s to help with how far back i can test so I can get more consistent results but I still need to figure out where I’m going wrong. Am I doing too much? Too many indicators, too many thresholds, over complicating it? If anybody has any tips or suggestions or any idea and what other people are doing Thats similar to me that would be very much appreciated. I made this on vs code with sonnet. Learned how to do this from my computer engineer brother so sorry if this seems a little ameture haha.

Too bad he’s into sports betting tho. Just to clarify im only asking to improve my own strategy, not use somebody’s else’s. 

Thanks for any help!!

comments.author: Strange-Pin-2717
comments.body: Try XG boost on the data and see which indicator / custom indicator is actually worth making sense.

replies.author: epicskyes
replies.body:
This is good advice I made an ensemble stack with GPU preprocessing: cudf / cudf.pandas
	•	GPU base models: 3–4 XGBoost regressors on cuda:1
	•	Optional GPU challenger: cuml.LinearRegression
	•	Meta-model: CPU positive Lasso
	•	Explainability: XGBoost GPU SHAP
Helps find what’s working and what isn’t after cpu strat run through

replies.author: CelerySpecial
replies.body: What would you target as the output?

replies.author: epicskyes
replies.body: Variations that perform better than your current inputs

replies.author: Individual_Mind_5523
replies.body: hey i’m super sorry to ask this . but i want to dm you with a question but it won’t allow me to for some odd reason . it says i can’t message this account. could you perchance help me out?

comments.author: Opening-Berry-6041
comments.body: Given you built this whole setup yourself with Sonnet and learned from your brother, how do you even begin to decide which indicators are actually worth the computational overhead and might be worth pursuing further instead of just adding more complexity?

replies.author: CelerySpecial
replies.body: I stated out with all the indicators I used for trading on my own. And then added whatever Claude was wanted to add to it. But most was just what I thought should be used from my experience in trading of Thats what youre asking. I felt like adding more complexity was kind of watering down the whole thing so I’m not totally sure if I should keep in that direction or refine the strategy to a smaller set of indicators

comments.author: Jeffieboi123
comments.body: Nike has made the turn, time to acquire

comments.author: melbkiwi
comments.body: I think part of the problem is that backtesting gives answers fast, and that can become addictive in itself. You tweak something, rerun it, and get instant feedback. Live testing is the complete opposite. It’s slow, boring, and often unclear for long stretches. A lot of people probably aren’t just fighting strategy issues, they’re fighting the fact that reality moves much slower than optimisation does

replies.author: CelerySpecial
replies.body:
I agree. Thats sounds good I’ll try more EAs. That’s a good point, I’ll look into my entry and exit rules. 
Ya I actually made a new branch that i deleted a lot of stuff on to kind of step back and rework what I was doing. I’ll try these ideas

replies.author: melbkiwi
replies.body:
In my case I’ve split the job across 3 EAs so I can isolate where the problem really is.

The trade EA is the one that actually looks for setups and places the trades.

The management EA looks after open trades afterwards, things like stop management, protection, and exit behaviour.

The memory EA is more of an observer. It records and tracks what was happening around the trade so I can study context and work out whether the issue was entry, management, or the market conditions themselves.

That split makes debugging a lot easier than trying to fix one big all-in EA without knowing which part is actually failing.

replies.author: CelerySpecial
replies.body: Ya that Seems like a well thought out way to do it. I kinda just have everything done at once for the original strategy

comments.author: BackTesting-Queen
comments.body: It's great to see your dedication to improving your strategy. It's not uncommon to feel overwhelmed when you're using a multitude of indicators and thresholds. Sometimes, simplifying your approach can lead to more consistent results. For instance, focusing on a few key indicators that align with your trading style might be beneficial. Also, consider the psychological aspects of trading. Even the most sophisticated backtesting tools, like WealthLab, can't account for human behavior. Reflect on your trades, understand your motivations, and ensure they align with your strategy. Lastly, don't be too hard on yourself. Trading is a journey, and every step, even the missteps, are opportunities to learn and grow. Keep going!

replies.author: CelerySpecial
replies.body: I definitely don’t intend on slowing down. And i agree, long journey. Great advice

comments.author: Imaginary_Past_1382

comments.author: Forsaken_Leader_8
comments.body: You're likely suffering from 'Dimensionality Curse.' When you score too many indicators (RSI, MACD, EMAs) across multiple timeframes, the signals start to contradict each other, which is exactly why your trades are hitting the hold limit—the 'conviction' disappears mid-trade. The most successful models in 2026 have actually moved away from 'indicator sweeping' and toward **multimodal sentiment.** I found that instead of adding a 10th indicator, adding a single AI-driven sentiment layer from [signalwhisper.com](http://signalwhisper.com) changed everything. It filters out the technical noise by telling you if the 'Institutional Data' or 'Social Sentiment' actually supports the breakout. Less is almost always more in quant trading.
